author: @PeterRichardsandYoureNot
text: I wouldn’t go 10 years back, on supply, but there is definitely a bell curve for pricing where the second we have bad news, all prices go up. And then oil barrels fall off steeply, but the catch up on the back side of the bell curve is where the stations and refineries make their most profits, so they are not incentivized to drop pump prices quickly. This is where the government needs to step in and force them to follow the curve and not drag their feet.  I did research on this about 5 years ago and mapped it all out. It is insane how much money they make on the price fluctuations. They always claim “we make it up on volume and only price for a few cents profit on each gallon.” Yeah, sure.  Which is exactly how you can afford the land lease for your station, brand new pump gear required to stay up to date on regulations, among many other overhead drains. And no….they don’t make it up in selling beef jerky.
